title: Protection from Redundancy (Pregnancy and Family Leave) Act 2023
chapters: 4 sections. A short but significant Act that amends the ERA 1996 and the MAPLE Regulations 1999 to EXTEND the period during which pregnant employees and those returning from maternity, adoption, or shared parental leave have PRIORITY ACCESS to suitable alternative vacancies in a redundancy situation. The protection doubles the effective period from approximately one year to approximately two years. Implemented by the Maternity Leave, Adoption Leave and Shared Parental Leave (Amendment) Regulations 2024 (SI 2024/XXX), in force 6 April 2024.

parliamentaryOrigin.bill: Protection from Redundancy (Pregnancy and Family Leave) Bill [Private Members' Bill]
parliamentaryOrigin.introducedBy: Dan Jarvis MP (Labour) — Private Members' Bill, backed by the Government following the 2019 consultation on pregnancy and maternity discrimination
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementNotes: In force from 6 April 2024. Applies to: (a) pregnancies notified to the employer on or after 6 April 2024, (b) maternity/adoption leave ending on or after 6 April 2024, (c) shared parental leave of 6+ consecutive weeks starting on or after 6 April 2024. The amendments are implemented through the Maternity Leave, Adoption Leave and Shared Parental Leave (Amendment) Regulations 2024 which amend the MAPLE Regulations 1999, the Paternity and Adoption Leave Regulations 2002, and the Shared Parental Leave Regulations 2014.

precedingDocuments.title: MAPLE Regulations 1999 Regulation 10 (existing redundancy protection during maternity leave)
precedingDocuments.significance: The EXISTING protection: where an employee on maternity leave, shared parental leave, or adoption leave is selected for redundancy, the employer must offer a suitable alternative vacancy if one exists. The employee gets PRIORITY — they must be offered the role before any other at-risk employee. The 2023 Act EXTENDS this protection to cover the period BEFORE maternity leave (during pregnancy) and AFTER leave (for 18 months from birth/placement).

precedingDocuments.title: BEIS Consultation on Pregnancy and Maternity Discrimination (2019)
precedingDocuments.significance: The 2019 consultation found that pregnancy and maternity discrimination remained widespread. BEIS reported that 6 months would be an adequate 'return to work' protection period. The Government committed to extending protection from the point of pregnancy notification to 6 months after the return from maternity leave.

precedingDocuments.title: EHRC/BIS Research: Pregnancy and Maternity-Related Discrimination (2016)
precedingDocuments.significance: The landmark research finding that approximately 54,000 women per year lose their jobs due to pregnancy or maternity — through redundancy, dismissal, or being treated so badly they feel forced to leave. This research directly informed the legislative response.

leadingCases.name: No reported case law as at March 2026 — the extended protection is less than 2 years old
leadingCases.principle: The extended protection came into force on 6 April 2024. As at March 2026, no reported tribunal decisions have tested the new provisions. SURVEILLANCE: monitor for the first decisions on: what constitutes 'suitable alternative vacancy' in the context of the extended protection, how tribunals handle competing priority claims (multiple protected employees), and the interaction with collective redundancy consultation.
leadingCases.significance: The first test cases will likely involve employers who failed to offer a suitable alternative vacancy to a pregnant employee or a returning mother, particularly where the employer was unaware of the extended protection.

sections.title: Three protected periods — pregnancy, maternity/adoption leave, and return from leave
sections.summary: The Act creates THREE overlapping protected periods during which the employee has priority access to suitable alternative vacancies in a redundancy situation. Period 1: PREGNANCY — from the date the employee notifies the employer of pregnancy until maternity leave begins (or, if the pregnancy ends before 24 weeks, until 2 weeks after the end of the pregnancy). Period 2: MATERNITY/ADOPTION LEAVE — the existing MAPLE Reg.10 protection during the leave itself (unchanged). Period 3: RETURN FROM LEAVE — from the end of maternity/adoption leave until 18 months from the date of birth or placement. For shared parental leave (6+ consecutive weeks without prior maternity/adoption leave): from the end of SPL until 18 months from birth/placement. The combined effect: a woman who notifies her employer at 12 weeks, takes a year of maternity leave, and returns — has priority protection for approximately 2 years.
sections.text:
THE THREE PROTECTED PERIODS:

PERIOD 1 — PREGNANCY (NEW):
— Starts: the day the employee notifies the employer of pregnancy (on or after 6 April 2024).
— Ends: the day maternity leave begins (or 2 weeks after the end of pregnancy if pregnancy ends before 24 weeks — miscarriage/termination).
— Stillbirth after 24 weeks: the employee is entitled to full maternity leave and the protection continues through Periods 2 and 3.
— During this period: if the employee is selected for redundancy, the employer MUST offer any suitable alternative vacancy before offering it to any other at-risk employee.

PERIOD 2 — MATERNITY/ADOPTION/SPL (EXISTING — UNCHANGED):
— Covers the entire period of maternity leave, adoption leave, or shared parental leave.
— MAPLE Reg.10: the employer must offer a suitable alternative vacancy to the employee on leave before making them redundant.
— The vacancy must be suitable and appropriate, on terms not substantially less favourable.
— The employee does NOT need to apply or compete — they must be OFFERED the role.

PERIOD 3 — RETURN FROM LEAVE (NEW):
— Starts: the day after the last day of maternity/adoption leave.
— Ends: 18 months from the date of birth or the date of the child's placement for adoption.
— This includes any time spent on leave — so an employee returning from a year of maternity leave has approximately 6 months of additional protection.
— For SPL (6+ consecutive weeks, no prior maternity/adoption leave): the protected period ends 18 months from birth/placement.

PRACTICAL TIMELINE EXAMPLE:
— Employee notifies pregnancy at 12 weeks (late June): Period 1 begins.
— Baby due in December, maternity leave starts November: Period 1 ends, Period 2 begins.
— Employee takes 52 weeks maternity leave, returns November next year: Period 2 ends, Period 3 begins.
— 18 months from birth (June the following year): Period 3 ends.
— TOTAL PROTECTION: approximately 24 months from pregnancy notification to end of Period 3.

WHAT 'PRIORITY' MEANS:
(1) The employer must offer a suitable alternative vacancy to the protected employee BEFORE offering it to any other at-risk employee — even if the other employee is better qualified or more experienced.
(2) The protected employee gets 'first refusal' — they do not need to compete.
(3) If the employee unreasonably refuses a suitable alternative, the employer is not in breach.
(4) If no suitable alternative exists, the employee can still be made redundant — but the employer must demonstrate a genuine redundancy situation and a fair process.
(5) This is NOT a ban on making protected employees redundant — it is PRIORITY ACCESS to redeployment.

AUTOMATIC UNFAIR DISMISSAL:
If the employer fails to offer a suitable alternative vacancy to a protected employee, the dismissal is AUTOMATICALLY UNFAIR — no qualifying service period required. This makes the risk of litigation very high.
sections.keyPrinciple: The extended protection creates a practical obligation on HR managers to TRACK protected employees throughout the organisation. Before any redundancy exercise, the HR manager must identify: who is pregnant, who is on maternity/adoption/SPL, and who has returned from leave within the last 18 months from birth/placement. ALL of these employees have priority access to suitable alternative vacancies. Failure to offer a vacancy that exists is automatic unfair dismissal — no qualifying period, no cap on compensation for discrimination claims.
refs: ERA 1996 s.47C (detriment in relation to pregnancy/maternity)
MAPLE Regulations 1999 Reg.10 (redundancy priority during maternity leave — as amended)
Maternity Leave, Adoption Leave and Shared Parental Leave (Amendment) Regulations 2024
EqA 2010 s.18 (pregnancy/maternity discrimination — no qualifying period, uncapped compensation)

regulatoryGuidance.title: Government guidance on redundancy protection during pregnancy and family leave
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: Government guidance on the new extended protection, including worked examples and employer responsibilities.

commonErrors: Not knowing which employees are in a protected period — the HR manager must maintain a tracker of: pregnant employees (from notification date), employees on maternity/adoption/SPL, and employees who have returned from leave (until 18 months from birth/placement). Without this tracker, the employer cannot comply

Treating priority access as a suggestion rather than a legal obligation — if a suitable alternative vacancy exists and a protected employee is at risk of redundancy, the employer MUST offer it to the protected employee BEFORE any other at-risk employee. This is not discretionary

Making a protected employee compete for a vacancy alongside other at-risk employees — the protected employee gets FIRST REFUSAL. They do not need to interview, score, or compete. If the vacancy is suitable, it must be offered to them

Not understanding that the protection starts from PREGNANCY NOTIFICATION, not from maternity leave — from 6 April 2024, the moment an employee notifies their employer of pregnancy, the priority protection is triggered. This can be many months before maternity leave begins

Failing to recognise the interaction with discrimination law — a redundancy that breaches the priority protection is not just automatically unfair dismissal (ERA 1996 s.99) but also likely pregnancy/maternity discrimination under the EqA 2010 s.18. Discrimination compensation is UNCAPPED

Not handling competing priority claims — as the number of protected employees increases, employers may face situations where multiple protected employees are at risk simultaneously and there is only one suitable alternative vacancy. The employer must assess who has the strongest claim — but there is no statutory hierarchy among protected employees

Assuming that pregnancy notification must be in writing — the 2019 consultation stated that notification could be 'orally or in writing'. Any form of notification triggers the protection. An employer who says 'you didn't tell us in writing' is on weak ground

pendingAmendments.instrument: First tribunal decisions on extended protection
pendingAmendments.status: Monitor for the first employment tribunal decisions testing the new provisions, particularly on competing priority claims and the definition of 'suitable alternative vacancy' in the context of the extended protection.
Monitor the ERA 2025 for any further amendments to the redundancy protection framework, including potential extension to other forms of family leave.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: MODERATE — possible further expansion of protected periods
